component_identification.content: Typical box contents: erasable sketch books (one per supported player), dry-erase markers, cleaning cloths, large prompt card deck/word list, and category die or selector depending edition; some versions include timer. Player count capacity varies by edition (classic vs party pack), but each active player must have one sketch book and marker. Before session start, verify all books erase cleanly and marker tips are usable.

core_game_loop.content: Each player starts with a secret prompt word and writes it in their sketch book. Books are passed; recipients draw the word, then pass again. Next recipient writes a guess describing the drawing, then pass continues alternating DRAW then GUESS every step. When books return to original owners, everyone reveals the full chain from original word through transformations. Optional points can be awarded, but many groups play mainly for comedic reveal quality.

detailed_rules.content: Distribute books/markers/cloths and choose prompt-selection method (card+number/category die in standard rules). Round start: each player records their secret word on first page, then all pass books in agreed direction. On each timer segment, players perform the required action for page type: if they see text, they draw that clue on next page; if they see drawing, they write a guess on next page. Only the most recent page should be visible to prevent future information leakage. Continue pass-rotate until each book is full and returns home. Reveal phase: each owner flips through their book sequence aloud, showing how message drifted over successive interpretations. Rule clarifications: drawings generally may not include letters or direct textual labels unless house variant allows minimal symbols; stick-figure quality is fully legal; time limit should be equal for all turns; late players stop when timer ends to preserve tempo. If scoring mode is used, apply one of the official/house methods consistently before play begins. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Telestrations can be played with or without formal scoring. In scored play, points are awarded according to selected system (commonly for correct guesses, successful transmission, or peer-voted favorite moments). In no-score party mode, game “win” is social and rounds end after reveal laughter. Session end is after agreed number of rounds/books; if using points, highest total wins. No hidden endgame triggers exist beyond planned round count.

beginner_strategy.content: When drawing, emphasize iconic shapes and relationships over detail—clarity beats artistry. As a guesser, write the most literal interpretation rather than joke answers if your group is scoring competitively. Keep pass direction and rhythm disciplined; delays break energy and cause page confusion. Teach first-time players with a mini demo chain so they understand alternating draw/guess pages. If everyone is new, start without scoring to reduce pressure and improve participation. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from telestrations.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: TELESTRATIONS - Official Rulebook (6 Player Family Pack)
